title: Lorraine by Charles Kingsley — Nature Poetry Lines
content:
“ARE you ready for your steeplechase, Lorraine, Lorraine, Lorree?
Barum, Barum, Barum, Barum, Barum, Barum, Baree.
You’re booked to ride your capping race to-day at Coulterlee,
You’re booked to ride Vindictive, for all the world to see,
To keep him straight, and keep him first, and win the run for me.”
Barum, Barum, Barum, Barum, Barum, Barum, Baree.

She clasp’d her newborn baby, poor Lorraine, Lorraine, Lorrèe,
meaning: "Lorraine" by Charles Kingsley is a nature poem consisting of 28 lines. The poet explores themes of nature through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "“ARE you ready for your steeplechase, Lorraine, Lorraine, Lorree? Barum, Barum, Barum, Barum, Barum,...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Charles Kingsley's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
